speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 4 of the interview with Liz Manning, March 8, 1996. Were Jews able to gain access to medical treatment in this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, no. There were Jewish doctors. And if it was something that involved something where no special outside treatment was necessary, you could go to somebody that-- if he wasn't sent away yet and you knew where he lived. You could ask him and he would say, you have a fever or medication. They were not allowed to write-- they were not allowed to write prescription or anything. And there was one Jewish hospital, which became a sort of a refugee point, transshipment point after the war, the Rothschild Hospital, which had always been a Jewish hospital. But they were-- they had no equipment. And they had no medication. They, they had the-- and some of the big doctors from the university and from all the fancy hospital, if they weren't sent away, they were there. But by-- in 1941, '40, '41 there were still a few. But by '42, '43, there was practically nobody left because everybody was gone. They had sent them all away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the way we became involved with this medical disaster was that my mother was at the time-- how old was she, 50, something like that? And she had some fibroid tumors. And they started to bleed, and she had a vaginal hemorrhage. And she was sitting on a-- on a thing-- on a pail in the house, and she was bleeding. And she said to me to go get the doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew of a doctor that we had gone to his office. He was about two blocks from the house. And I ran there. And he had office hours. He was there. And he didn't-- I don't know, whatever. And, and, and I explained it to him. So he said, all right, little girl. I'll come with you. And he said to his people he was going to be back. He was just going to see whether he could stop that woman's bleeding. And we walked out. And about halfway to my house, either he remembered me, or, or maybe I said something-- I honestly do not remember. He found out I was Jewish. And he said he cannot treat a Jewish woman. He would lose his license. I said, please, please, my mommy needs you. He said, no. And he left me stand there. He said he cannot go. He is not going. He says, I am not going to the house of a Jewish woman. And he went back to his office.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was afraid to go home because my mother had said to me I have to-- I have to bring back the doctor. She says, be sure he comes with you. You have to bring him back, OK? And I was afraid because my mother was going to be angry at me. And I, I, I, I didn't dare go home. So I took my, my brother. He was playing in front of the house. And I took him, and we went to the trolley car stop, and we waited for my father to come home from work, wherever he was. I don't know. I guess he must have still been working someplace because he wasn't there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he came off the, the-- that must have been maybe an hour and a half, two hours. And-- but I was afraid to go home 'cause I wasn't-- hadn't done what I was told to do. When he saw us standing there so dejectedly, he knew something was wrong. So he asked what it was, and we told him. And he-- and then he ran home. And I don't know what he did because they told-- they wouldn't let us into the house, you know. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow or other, something must have happened, because he tried-- then he tried to get her into a hospital. Of course he couldn't get her in anywheres. And it was some time before he got her into the, the Rothschild Hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when she came there, the doctor, he was a primarius. He had been some big shot in some hospital, fancy hospital, sanatorium, or something. His name was [? Schiffman. ?] I remember him, his name. And, and, and he said, what do people expected me? He, he, he, he was-- my father told me, you know, he told me afterwards he was screaming at him. He says, what do people expect of me? He says, I am not-- I am not a god. This woman doesn't have any blood left. What am I supposed to do with her? Blood, of course, was not-- your product was not available. So, anyway, my mother was in the-- in the hospital for, I don't know, two, three days, and she died. She had gangrene. My-- we were not allowed to go to the hospital. I never saw her after she left the house. We were not able to let children into a hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father came to visit her. And she twisted-- he had a vest, a three-piece suit, and a, a, a jacket. And she was in such pain she twisted all the buttons off his-- off his clothing, off the vest and off the thing because she was in such pain that, that-- she had no blood. She was dry. And, and, and, and, and then she-- there was so little blood and so little liquid, I guess they-- I don't know. She died of a blood clot in her lungs. That was the official death certificate. That's what it said. And I think she died of bleeding to death like a dog that somebody throws in a corner. She was killed because she was Jewish. And it hurts just as much today as if she had been gassed or been in a concentration camp. Because she died of the same thing that all the people that were gassed died, for being born with the wrong religion according to somebody's eyes. And I had to live with this. I realized that there was absolutely nothing I can do. I always understood that. And I understand it today. But I had to live with the fact that I let her sit there for two hours and I was afraid to go home for many, many years before I was able to work it out. And it wasn't anything I did wrong. It was something I did because I didn't know what else to do. But it, it was very difficult on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- and the older I get, the more I understand about the world and everything, the angrier I get. And I am-- I live-- I mean, I-- something happened to me. I'll tell you about a few months ago, actually. It wasn't much. It was about last year, eight, nine months ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in a building rage. I was forever angry. And I was angry at the world. I was angry at being cheated out of my life, of never having a childhood, of having a twisted body, of losing my mother, of losing my family, of losing my life, of losing my background. I thought that I would be there forever, and I will take my children to these places and show them of, of losing everything. And I was angry. And when, when you talk to people-- when I came here, I remember in the beginning, I didn't know what to do. And I applied certain jobs were not available to Jews. You couldn't become a waitress in Schrafft's. Schrafft's didn't hire Jews in 1947.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to be a waitress. But that was-- somebody said there is a job there. What did I know? Strange country, strange language, no background-- so I went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I figured, I don't know. I'll do it for two days. If I don't like it, I-- you know, the war is over. But they didn't want hiring Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- in, in, in, in 19-- I don't know when it was, in the-- around 1980 or something, that the cemetery in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Beth El in, in Cedar, Cedar Park-- what is it? In New Jersey. Oh, I forget the name of the thing. That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In New Jersey, in, in-- on, on-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cedar Park Beth El, the Jewish cemetery-- my father's gravestone was knocked over. It was vandalized. And I was hysterical for two days because I thought it was all over. And it never ends. And I was-- I, I lived in a total-- for many, many years. I-- I'm still-- the older I get, the angrier I get. And it's always perfectly all right. It's happening in Israel. It's happening in other places. People kill people. It's perfectly all right. If you're a Jew, you're vermin. And it doesn't count. If something happens to somebody else-- I'm sorry, I don't want to start doing this. All right, let's stop it. I'm-- I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm getting carried away.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it that your mother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died in 1941, November 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of changes occurred after her death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of changes occurred after her death for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of changes? We lived  in the same place. I worked in the same place. Instead of my mother cooking for me, my aunt cooked for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of my mother sleeping in her bed, she had the bed-- the bed. My father slept in the bed now. Because the bed that he had slept in-- they didn't have a double bed [? that this-- ?] you know? So he slept in the bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for, for-- we had a Torah in the house for 30 days. And we, we wonder-- you were practically not-- you know, because they said Kaddish there every day for 30 days, and, and-- in a little cupboard they built for that. And, and life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father continue to live a religious life throughout all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was religious all the time. As a matter of fact, my father was more religious after the war than he was before the war. Because my father felt that he did not always 100%. Every once in awhile, when he was younger especially, maybe before he married and had children, he was doing certain things that were not according to the book. I'm sure he did quite a lot of them, considering who he was. And he felt that God had punished him because he did not [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I kept saying to him, we, we had-- that was the only-- I adored my father. And I was Daddy's girl. He adored me. But he could not-- I could not understand how he could place his faith in something that there is nobody home. Well, where? What? Nothing happened. If there is-- well, I, I don't-- It's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to hurt anybody's feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if there is a, a divine presence, it would not have let happen what did. And if we are so unimportant in the bigger scheme of things that we can suffer like that, I don't want to pray to him, and I don't want to be friends with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When I needed him-- I'm sorry-- He wasn't there. [CRYING] And the only thing, I lost it. And I, I said to my father, how can you pray? Look what happened to Mama. Look what happened to your mother. Look what happened to our aunts. Look what happened to everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are dead. They were killed like dogs, when we understood how they had all died. Nobody came back. They were all-- they all disappeared from the face of the earth, maybe 80 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: How can you pray? He says, you don't understand. And I wanted to hit him. And I felt terrible about having these feelings about my father. But I still don't understand. How long did you continue to live in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came-- we came back with the transport in, in July. We had this typhoid epidemic. Oh, I-- do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean beforehand.

speaker: interviewee
text: --want me to tell me you the-- we were sent to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that-- we were sent to Theresienstadt in November, 1943. We came in there. And there is something that is worth listening to. We came in there, and we, we had to line up outside on the platform, and and-- for the-- for the SS to inspect us, you know, the crop, the-- what came in. And we're walking, and all of a sudden, the head of the-- the head of the Theresienstadt Concentration Camp at that time was somebody named Oberschaffer Rahm-- Obersturmfuhrer, whatever, over-something. And his name was Rahm, and-- Karl, Karl Rahm. And he-- and all of a sudden-- my father and-- we were standing there next to one another. And all of a sudden, my father talks through his, you know, through clenched teeth. He says, oh, my God. He says, we are all dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what happened, Papa? What happened? He says, oh, my God. So he tells me the following. Excuse me. He tells me the following story.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a business that was making-- he was in the carpentry, cabinet-making business. He was making showcases for stores and furniture, and windows, and things like that, storefronts and things. And he had-- he, he, he subcontracted out the other trades. And he subcontract out some work to a-- to a locksmith-- showcase locks, store locks, door locks, et cetera. And they sent a-- an apprentice, a, a young fellow. I don't know. I think my father was at the time 30, and this guy was maybe 19 or 20, something like that. And he sent somebody in. And my father didn't like the way that this young locksmith apprentice had done the work. That was-- he said he was too sloppy, and it wasn't any good. And he said to him to do it over because it wasn't acceptable this way. And he said to my father with the big, English blond officer's mustache there, he said to him, for this lousy Jew, it's good enough. And my father picked up his hand and slapped his face good and hard. And he says, when I say it's good enough, it's good enough. And you don't tell me. And that was it. And he spoke to the contractor. And he never sent him back again. And that was the end of it. And when we were-- you know already, of course, that was him. So when we were walking along there, he-- my father told me this story. I says, Papa, it's was what-- it was like 18-- it was, like, 20 years ago or 18 years ago. He won't remember you anymore. He says-- I said, nah, he's stupid. He won't remember you. He's not Jewish, I said. He's stupid. Anyway, we're walking, and he comes up. And he walks up. And he walks past us one step. And all of a sudden, he steps back. You know, the rear-- the leg-- the rear leg picks up and falls down again. And he comes back, and he looks at my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to him, Sussman, you're a Jew? And my father sort of sinks inside, oh my God. And he clicks his heels. And he says, Ja wohl, Herr Obersturmfuhrer. And he looks at him, and he shakes his head. And he wanders on-- wanders on. And after that, he looked out for him. Why do you think it was that he looked out for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he respected the fact that he didn't take any-- I, I don't know why. Do I know what things goes on in the head of a Nazi? Maybe he looked out for him because he had a personal connection. You know, he, he was his house Jew. How do I know? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe he had respected him for, for behaving the way he did. Maybe he respected him from before, and he liked him. And he thought, this is not a real Jew because the way he behaves. He's one of us. He's a good person. Well, who knows what goes on in the mind of some-- somebody like that, an abomination like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so he-- all through, through the time that we were in Theresienstadt, we were never on a transport. And they went on all the time. The-- Theresienstadt was only a place-- it was a transshipment point. People came, and they were sent from there. People were sent from, from, from, from Vienna, from, from Germany, from Prague, or from Czechoslovakia, from Austria, everywhere to Theresienstadt-- from Holland. And from Theresienstadt, they shipped them east.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances that led to your deportation from Vienna to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Kuchman lost his job in the Gestapo. And the guy that came after him no longer had any interest or wasn't even-- I'm sure he was not aware that our name was never allowed on any list. So when the next-- we knew when he came. We were put on the list, and we had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it? And who was with you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was it? It was in the, the first week of November 1943. And somebody came to our house at about 9 o'clock in the morning. And he says, you have three hours. You're in the transport. And we wanted-- it, it was-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] That's downstairs. That's Gregory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm sorry. I lost my thought. Edit it out. Anyway, he came-- he came to the house, and we had to pack. And whenever I wanted to take this or that, he would say to me-- he would say to me, never mind. You won't need that. Because sometimes they would tell people not to take certain things because they, they would come afterwards. We had to go and leave the house open, no keys, everything, and they would come and clean it out. They were also Jews, but it doesn't make any difference.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came, and then he, he told us to, to, to, to-- and we had to pack. And I took whatever I could. My father had a, a, a rucksack, which was about the size of a grown man, which came down to his knees. And he had it made out of sail cloth. And he took this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took a suitcase. And I took a rucksack. And my brother took a rucksack and a suitcase. When we came to Theresienstadt, they take our-- they took our suitcases away from us. The only thing we kept were the rucksacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to the rest of your family in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time we left, the only one that was still-- everybody else had been sent away. The only one-- my-- the only one that was still left was my, my, my Aunt [? Teta ?] with her husband. Somehow or other, they overlooked him. But he came a week after we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had any other members of your family been sent to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all of them. Well, my-- no. My father's oldest brother, the one that used to live in Luckenbach, he, his wife, and his, his son went to-- on a transport. They were going to go to Israel, and they got stuck in Zagreb. And they were in a concentration camp there in Yugoslavia. And they were killed there. And my father's sister managed to get out-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --in 1938. Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. In 1938-- it's downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that-- she got to Israel in 1938. She was on an illegal transport. She managed to get there. One of the, the-- my father's-- one of the brothers from, from Leipzig managed to get to England. And he was interned as a German national on the Isle of Man as a Jew in England during the war. Speaks volumes for the state of the world. And, and everybody else was sent away. One uncle managed to get to Poland. And, and he was in, in, in Lemberg, which was Lwow. And then after the war, we found out Lwow was the Russian side. But he was-- did not survive. His wife and his son were sent-- were sent-- they were-- they were all sent away. Every week, a thousand people were sent away, week after week, after week, after week, after week for two years until nobody was left. And you had no recourse. And you had no opportunity. And you had no anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt was a fortress that was built by the-- it was named in honor of Maria Theresa. It had a garrison of horse soldiers in it. It was about 40 kilometers outside-- northwest of Prague in-- very close to a place called Litomerice, which was on the other side of the Elbe. Theresienstadt was on a river called the Eger. And the Elbe-- on the other side of the Elbe was the Sudetenland. It was, like, a 20-minute walk from Theresienstadt over the fields, which we did after the war when we could get out. But-- so that was in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we came there, my father and my brother were assigned to one of the buildings that used to be horse stables, where they had people live in these three, you know, wooden, three-layered-- three-level things on sacks of straw. Everybody had one narrow cot-like thing about the width of this. You know, 30 inches wide and 6 feet long. How did the camp appear to you when you first arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a very old place with wall-to-wall people. There, there, there, there were 3,000 people living in Theresienstadt. It was a little town. They had moats, and a city wall, an underground thing, you know, passages. It was a fortress from 1600 something. Maria Theresa was the empress from 1640 to 1680. So it was a long time ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was very-- they had a few people there-- 3,000. And then sometimes, there were 60,000 people there. So it was wall-to-wall people and everybody-- a room like this would house maybe 30 people, with layers, you know, along there, and one across the end-- one on top of the other. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was it. So in Theresienstadt, I was assigned to, to work in something called the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We were-- we were-- mica, which is a, a rock which splinters. It's like, oh, I don't know. It's, it's-- they use the, the isinglass, you know, the thing in front of, of a-- of kerosene stoves, those glass little things. That is stone, and that can be split. And they wanted it for aircraft insulation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a instrument, something like a nail file with a knife at the end. And we had to split it. And they gave us something. They-- we had little examples, so many micrometers. And so, you know, parts of a millimeter, very thin-- they wanted it for aircraft insulation. And they used it. And they, they-- we had to split it. That was the Jewish job. I had to sit. And they had barracks. And we were sitting on this table all day long and do this, split rocks in two. And it turned out after the war, they never used it. They found sacks and sacks of that stored in the catacombs in Theresienstadt. But this is neither here nor there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there separate barracks for women and men?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- no, the men lived in the-- in, in, in this-- these stables. Women lived in-- men and women did-- there were separate assignment things. I lived in the barracks. I, I was assigned to something called the west barracks, which were five barracks on the outside of town. And we were living in three. And there were-- was an epidemic of bedbugs. I mean, there were trillions of them. I used to come down. And I lived on the third floor because I was young. I could climb the ladders. I lived on the third level. And we used to come down in the morning. And I used to shake out-- bend over and shake out my hair because it was full of bugs. And we didn't-- we didn't have anything. You would-- sometimes, you, you would take a, a, a-- we got-- our ration was a slice of bread about an inch thick across a loaf this wide, you know, like this, a day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 2 ounces-- 2 and 1/2 ounces of margarine a week, 2 and 1/2 ounces of sugar a week. In the morning, you got something which was called coffee. I don't know. We used to call it-- I don't want to say what we called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what was it that you called it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We called it nigger spit because it was dark, light-- sort of light-ish, white-ish brown, watery type of a thing. I have no idea what was in it. I never ate it. And they had something else that they gave us, which was a sort of a barley soup. It was sort of a gruel, a very watery, gray gruel. And that was the soup. And I never ate it because somebody said they put saltpeter in it. And they, they wanted to keep us down. I suppose there were a lot of people there. And I never ate the soup because it looked disgusting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were always old people standing there next to the windows where we would collect our food. And says, are you taking the soup? And I would say, no. And then somebody would say, they-- would, would see we would come. And they say, are you going to eat? No. So we would peel our potatoes and give them the peels. What other kinds of food did they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two potatoes. Once a week, we got a, a yeast dumpling. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had two-- they had something they called meat. It was a sort of a gluey sauce, which we got about two ounces of. That was supposed to-- it was called goulash. I don't know where-- there definitely was no meat in there. I don't know what it was. And that was the, the, the ration. It was the coffee in the morn. And, and at night, we got a plate of soup, thick soup, potato soup. A plate of this-- and it was gluey, but it was food. And for, for lunch, we got this, this either potato or this watery gruel soup. Either potato it was or, or, or, or a dumpling once a week, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and this glue sauce. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about condiments? Like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I say this was it, you have to do-- this was it. But there were-- there were two big barrels. I don't know whether you heard this before. That's why you're asking. There were two big barrels which were standing on the street corner. One was a bread spread, and one was mustard. And you could take as much as you wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had-- somebody had sent me a package with a, a 2-pound jar of jam. And I had the jar, and that was a treasure. I had a dish, which wasn't like the one that I had to have been cleaned each day for food, right? So I, I had that. And I went there every single day, and I filled up that thing with mustard. And I ate it [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with a teaspoon every day. And that's what I lived on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the, the, the margarine and the sugar, they were trade items. You didn't eat those. You bought yourself a pillow, a blanket. For a weeks' worth of sugar and margarine, you could get a blanket. Or you could get a coat, or your-- or a sweater, or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything special that you remember trading for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember trading for-- I bought-- I got myself a, a, a, a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a coat. I was lucky. I had a coat. It was November, so I had a coat. And I bought myself a warm sweater. And my shoes wore out. I had no soles at all. I just had the rims. And I bought myself a pair of shoes. Unfortunately, they were three sizes too big. And that's how I ruined my feet because I didn't have decent shoes to wear.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now and change the tape.
